<![CDATA[Stories by Kris Witalewski on Medium]]> https://medium.com/@kriswitalewski?source=rss-f3bf0cdf7837------2 https://cdn-images-1.medium.com/fit/c/150/150/1*72DMwcv05LmbbXMQ9lkjWQ.jpeg Stories by Kris Witalewski on Medium https://medium.com/@kriswitalewski?source=rss-f3bf0cdf7837------2 Medium Thu, 13 Dec 2018 14:04:23 GMT <![CDATA[Reuse Component logic with custom React Hooks]]> https://itnext.io/reuse-component-logic-with-custom-react-hooks-a8f4bbf8c4aa?source=rss-f3bf0cdf7837------2 https://medium.com/p/a8f4bbf8c4aa Tue, 11 Dec 2018 10:13:40 GMT 2018-12-11T13:46:33.590Z

In my previous post I described how to convert a Class Component to a Function Component with the same state and lifecycle using React Hooks. One of the major reasons for using hooks, though, is new ways to reuse component logic — without Higher Order Components, Render Props etc.

Example

Let’s consider a Higher Order Component which given a reference to a HTML node and function to load more data turns any list to one with infinite scroll:

hooks-demo-part-2 - CodeSandbox

Here’s the relevant code:

And here’s how it’s used in the Component:

Rewriting the Higher Order Component as a Custom Hook

Let’s now try to use React Hooks to implement the exact same behaviour as a Custom Hook.

Imports and definition

We no longer need to import Component, instead we’ll need the useState and useEffect hooks. And we don’t even need to import React as there will no longer by any JSX to be rendered in our Custom Hook.

Next, define our Custom Hook as a function:

Initial state

We will need state to differentiate between the first run and subsequent runs (think: componentDidMount vs componentDidUpdate) in useEffect later:

Check if more items need to be loaded

The shouldLoadMoreItems function stays largely the same with two exceptions. The trivial one is that we now reference function arguments rather than Component props. The less trivial is that isLoading cannot be simply passed to the function hook as a value, because the value will change in-between renders as more data is loaded. Therefore, we’ll be passing it as a function returning current isLoading value and executing it in shouldLoadMoreItems like this:

Window event handler

The event handles stays exactly the same except for references to function arguments replacing references to Component props:

Lifecycle hooks

Next, we need to rewrite componentDidMount and componentWillUnmount with useEffect:

Render

And that’s it. We won’t be rendering anything, so that little piece of code goes away completely:

Final result

Here’s the complete function code:

Using the Custom Hook

In the list Component, also rewritten as a Function Component, we use the Custom Hook we created just as we would use any of the built-in hooks:

You can view the complete code, both with Higher Order Component and with Custom Hook in the GitHub repo.

More on Hooks

This article is the second part of my short series on React Hooks:

  • Add state and lifecycle methods to Function Components with React Hooks
  • Reuse Component logic with custom React Hooks

Reuse Component logic with custom React Hooks was originally published in ITNEXT on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

]]> <![CDATA[Add state and lifecycle methods to Function Components with React Hooks]]> https://itnext.io/add-state-and-lifecycle-methods-to-function-components-with-react-hooks-8e2bdc44d43d?source=rss-f3bf0cdf7837------2 https://medium.com/p/8e2bdc44d43d Fri, 09 Nov 2018 22:18:52 GMT 2018-12-11T10:15:07.753Z

Let’s consider a simple to do list component:

Here’s the full code:

Nothing new or unusual so far. The component uses componentDidMount lifecycle method to fetch data from a remote source when first rendered and then shows a to do list. Items can be added, removed and marked as done — all this is achieved by means of updating the component state.

Rewriting the to do list as a Function Component

Let’s now try to use React Hooks to implement the exact same behaviour in a function component.

Imports and component definition

We no longer need to import Component, instead we’ll need the useState and useEffect hooks.

Next, define our Component as a Function rather than Class Component.

Constructor and initial state

Instead of defining initial state in a constructor, we will call the useState hook in the function body:

Run code when component first renders

In case you wonder why we add another field — initialized — to the Component state: notice that there is a single useEffect hook which can replace both componentDidMount and componentDidUpdate. As we only want to fetch the data once, we need to be able to differentiate between those 2 cases.

Update remaining references to state

All that is left now is to replace all other references to this.state and this.setState with the methods returned from useState hooks.

Along the way we’ll also fix references to markTodoAsDone, removeTodo and addNewTodo which will now be function constants rather than class methods, so they will no longer be referenced with this keyword.

Notice how in 2 out of 3 cases these updated functions fit into just a single line or two (with default Prettier config). Only when we update multiple state fields in one function, the code remains at comparable length and one might argue it becomes less coherent with separate calls replacing the single setState call.

Replace render with return

The final step is to return whatever was previously returned from the render method, again updating the references to state and helper functions. This is what the final code looks like:

And the two components, side by side:

Feel free to fork or download the CodeSandbox example, play around and see how those two compare.

What about the other lifecycle methods?

As we learned earlier, useEffect doubles as a replacement for both componentDidMount and componentDidUpdate. Interestingly, a function returned from useEffect will be called whenever componentWillUpdate or componentWillUnmount would run in a Class Component. Take a look at the example:

I also added similar statements to the lifecycle methods of the Class Component. See how the log looks similar after opening a page with both components displayed:

If you are interested in accessing previous props in componentDidUpdate (in case of a component which actually has some), this question is explained in the Hooks FAQ.

Are there any differences?

Both components look and behave exactly the same. One interesting difference is in how the component state is actually stored.

State of the Class Component is exactly what we would expect it to be.

State of the Function Component, meanwhile, looks quite different.

Remember how exactly we defined our state in the Function Component?

Individual fields are stored as a chain of baseState which references the value and next, pointing to the next field, in order in which they are declared in the Function Component body. It probably won’t matter anyway, so just consider this as a piece of trivia… unless the testing frameworks won’t implement support for accessing Component state the usual way for Components making use of the setState hooks.

More on Hooks

This article is the first part of my short series on React Hooks:

  • Add state and lifecycle methods to Function Components with React Hooks

Add state and lifecycle methods to Function Components with React Hooks was originally published in ITNEXT on Medium, where people are continuing the conversation by highlighting and responding to this story.

]]> <![CDATA[Automatically switch between light and dark theme in macOS Mojave with Nightcall]]> https://medium.com/@kriswitalewski/automatically-switch-between-light-and-dark-theme-in-macos-mojave-with-nightcall-c3b7dd0797a1?source=rss-f3bf0cdf7837------2 https://medium.com/p/c3b7dd0797a1 Wed, 07 Nov 2018 11:32:04 GMT 2018-11-09T22:26:07.365Z

Dark mode in macOS Mojave is really nice. It changes not only the status bar, menu and dock (like in previous versions) but also backgrounds in system applications like Finder, Photos or Notes. Hopefully, other vendors will soon adjust their applications too so that most of them have a light and dark version of the UI.

Why doesn’t it switch automatically?

One thing I can’t really wrap my head around is why there is no option to toggle between light and dark modes automatically. When I code, I usually prefer to use a light UI theme in my IDE during the day but I switch it to dark theme after sunset. Along with a tool like f.lux it really makes working late easier on the eyes. It would be great if such switch could happen for all my user interfaces automatically.

Enter Nightcall

Lately I wrote a little application called Nightcall which does exactly that. After installation it checks your physical location based on nearby WiFi networks and your IP address and schedules theme changes on sunrise and sunset. In case you travel between timezoned, Nightcall has you covered by checking the location on each update and each time your computer boots. I also made a VS Code extension to go along.

Installing Nightcall

To install Nightcall you need to have yarn or npm installed first. Then just run yarn global add nightcall or npm i -g nightcall. That's it!
